title: Palabras pequeñas
description: A two-choice pop quiz for explicit training of high-frequency small Spanish words.
introMessage: Small words do big work. First know them directly, then recognize them in sentences. We will train both directions: Spanish to English and English to Spanish.
successMessage: Perfect streak. You knew every small word both ways, and that is exactly how strong reading starts.
failureMessage: That one is worth seeing again. Small words are learned by repetition, and the next round will make it stronger.

items.example: Voy a la escuela. = I go to school.
items.explanation: a often points toward a destination or marks where something is going.

items.example: Voy a la escuela. = I go to school.
items.explanation: a often points toward a destination or marks where something is going.

items.example: Soy de Finlandia. = I am from Finland.
items.explanation: de often shows origin, source, or belonging.

items.example: Soy de Finlandia. = I am from Finland.
items.explanation: de often shows origin, source, or belonging.

items.example: Vivo en Madrid. = I live in Madrid.
items.explanation: en marks where something is located or where something happens.

items.example: Vivo en Madrid. = I live in Madrid.
items.explanation: en marks where something is located or where something happens.

items.example: Estudio con mi amigo. = I study with my friend.
items.explanation: con means something or someone is included.

items.example: Estudio con mi amigo. = I study with my friend.
items.explanation: con means something or someone is included.

items.example: Tomo café sin azúcar. = I drink coffee without sugar.
items.explanation: sin means something is missing or not included.

items.example: Tomo café sin azúcar. = I drink coffee without sugar.
items.explanation: sin means something is missing or not included.

items.example: Camino por el parque. = I walk through the park.
items.explanation: por often marks a route, cause, exchange, or means.

items.example: Camino por el parque. = I walk through the park.
items.explanation: por often marks a route, cause, exchange, or means.

items.example: Pan y agua. = Bread and water.
items.explanation: y adds one thing to another.

items.example: Pan y agua. = Bread and water.
items.explanation: y adds one thing to another.

items.example: Padre e hijo. = Father and son.
items.explanation: e replaces y before words that start with an i sound.

items.example: Padre e hijo. = Father and son.
items.explanation: e replaces y before words that start with an i sound.

items.example: Café o té. = Coffee or tea.
items.explanation: o presents a choice between options.

items.example: Café o té. = Coffee or tea.
items.explanation: o presents a choice between options.

items.example: Siete u ocho. = Seven or eight.
items.explanation: u replaces o before words that start with an o sound.

items.example: Siete u ocho. = Seven or eight.
items.explanation: u replaces o before words that start with an o sound.

items.example: No tengo pan ni agua. = I have neither bread nor water.
items.explanation: ni adds another negative idea.

items.example: No tengo pan ni agua. = I have neither bread nor water.
items.explanation: ni adds another negative idea.

items.example: Creo que tienes razón. = I think that you are right.
items.explanation: que connects a thought, statement, comparison, or clause.

items.example: Creo que tienes razón. = I think that you are right.
items.explanation: que connects a thought, statement, comparison, or clause.

items.example: Ella se lava las manos. = She washes her hands.
items.explanation: se points the action back to he, she, they, or you formal.

items.example: Ella se lava las manos. = She washes her hands.
items.explanation: se points the action back to he, she, they, or you formal.

items.example: Yo me llamo Ana. = My name is Ana.
items.explanation: me points the action to I or me.

items.example: Yo me llamo Ana. = My name is Ana.
items.explanation: me points the action to I or me.

items.example: Tú te llamas Luis. = Your name is Luis.
items.explanation: te points the action to you.

items.example: Tú te llamas Luis. = Your name is Luis.
items.explanation: te points the action to you.

items.example: No lo entiendo. = I do not understand it.
items.explanation: lo can refer to a masculine thing, a male person, or a general it.

items.example: No lo entiendo. = I do not understand it.
items.explanation: lo can refer to a masculine thing, a male person, or a general it.

items.example: No la veo. = I do not see her.
items.explanation: la can refer to a feminine thing or person.

items.example: No la veo. = I do not see her.
items.explanation: la can refer to a feminine thing or person.

items.example: Veo los libros. = I see the books.
items.explanation: los goes with masculine plural nouns or masculine plural objects.

items.example: Veo los libros. = I see the books.
items.explanation: los goes with masculine plural nouns or masculine plural objects.

items.example: Veo las casas. = I see the houses.
items.explanation: las goes with feminine plural nouns or feminine plural objects.

items.example: Veo las casas. = I see the houses.
items.explanation: las goes with feminine plural nouns or feminine plural objects.

items.example: Tengo un libro. = I have a book.
items.explanation: un usually goes before a masculine singular noun.

items.example: Tengo un libro. = I have a book.
items.explanation: un usually goes before a masculine singular noun.

items.example: Tengo una casa. = I have a house.
items.explanation: una usually goes before a feminine singular noun.

items.example: Tengo una casa. = I have a house.
items.explanation: una usually goes before a feminine singular noun.
